content.text: Overview

Dashboard proliferation is the silent tax of a successful observability program. Every team adds panels, few teams retire them, and within two years the Grafana instance contains hundreds of dashboards that nobody owns, half of which show no data.
Ownership standards
prevent this by assigning a named team to every dashboard and running it through a lifecycle: author, review, active ownership, and eventual retirement.
This topic defines the review checklist, OWNERS file format, quarterly review process, and retirement criteria that keep a GenAI observability program clean and trustworthy.

attrs.alt: Dashboard lifecycle: Author → Review → Active Ownership → Retire

content.text: The OWNERS File
Every dashboard folder in the repository contains an
OWNERS
file. It declares who is responsible for the dashboards in that folder and feeds GitHub's CODEOWNERS enforcement.

# dashboards/teams/search-llm/OWNERS
approvers:
  - alice-observability-lead    # must approve all PRs to this folder
  - bob-tech-lead               # team tech lead
reviewers:
  - sre-oncall-rotation         # auto-assigned by GitHub CODEOWNERS
  - carol-ml-engineer
labels:
  team: search-llm
  audience: oncall              # oncall | lead | ml-eng | finance
  slo-linked: "yes"             # does this dashboard back an SLO?
  reviewed: "2025-Q3"           # quarter of last owner certification
The root
.github/CODEOWNERS
file maps dashboard paths to the approvers in each OWNERS file:

# .github/CODEOWNERS
dashboards/templates/          @observability-leads
dashboards/teams/search-llm/   @alice-observability-lead @bob-tech-lead
dashboards/teams/rag-service/  @alice-observability-lead @dan-rag-lead
dashboards/lib/                @observability-leads
This means no dashboard can be merged to
main
without approval from the designated observability lead
and
the relevant team lead. The structural separation between
templates/
lib/
, and
teams/
ensures the shared panel library has a single approver path.
Review Checklist
Every PR that adds or modifies a dashboard must pass a two-part review: an automated CI gate and a human review against a standard checklist.
CI Gate (Automated)
jsonnet
renders to valid Grafana JSON without errors
UID is set and unique across all dashboards in the repository
All panels have a
datasource
variable reference — no hardcoded datasource UIDs
No panel uses
instant
query mode for timeseries panels (common copy-paste bug)
OWNERS file exists and references at least one approver from
.github/CODEOWNERS

# .github/workflows/dashboard-review.yml (CI gate excerpt)
- name: Check UID uniqueness
  run: |
    python3 scripts/check_uids.py dashboards/
  # check_uids.py extracts "uid" from every rendered JSON
  # and exits non-zero if any UID appears more than once

- name: Check datasource references
  run: |
    for f in $(find dashboards/ -name '*.jsonnet'); do
      jsonnet -J vendor "$f" | python3 - <<'PY'
import sys, json
d = json.load(sys.stdin)
for p in d.get('panels', []):
    ds = p.get('datasource', {})
    if isinstance(ds, dict) and not ds.get('uid', '').startswith('$'):
        print(f"FAIL: panel '{p.get('title')}' has hardcoded datasource")
        sys.exit(1)
PY
    done
Human Review Checklist
Category
Check
Why it matters
Naming
Title matches template naming convention
Grafana search depends on consistent names
Naming
UID follows prefix scheme (genai-<type>-<team>-v1)
Alert links and bookmarks use UIDs
Queries
Rate windows use [5m] or wider — no [1m] on high-cardinality metrics
1m windows cause CPU spikes at the Prometheus level
Queries
All label filters reference template variables ($env, $model)
Without variable filters, panels show all environments combined
Cardinality
No group-by on high-cardinality labels (request_id, user_id, trace_id)
These explode the Prometheus time-series count
Units
Every panel has a unit set (s, tokens/s, currencyUSD, percentunit)
Unitless panels cause misreads during incidents
Thresholds
Stat and gauge panels have colour thresholds aligned with the SLO
Thresholds without SLO backing are noise
SLO alignment
If slo-linked: yes, panel queries match the SLO error budget burn calculation
Inconsistent queries undermine SLO trust
Audience
audience label matches the actual set of panels (no cost panels in oncall dashboard)
Wrong audience causes alert fatigue or missed signals
Quarterly Review Process
Dashboard content drifts even without changes. Metric names get renamed, services split, and context shifts. The
quarterly review
requires each dashboard owner to certify that their dashboards are still accurate.
The process runs automatically via a GitHub Action on the first Monday of each quarter:

# .github/workflows/quarterly-review.yml
name: Quarterly Dashboard Review
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 1-7 1,4,7,10 1'   # first Monday of Jan/Apr/Jul/Oct at 09:00 UTC
jobs:
  open-review-issues:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Find outdated OWNERS files
        id: outdated
        run: |
          python3 scripts/find_stale_owners.py dashboards/             --quarter $(date +%Y-Q%q)             --output stale.json
      - name: Open GitHub issues for stale dashboards
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const stale = require('./stale.json');
            for (const item of stale) {
              await github.rest.issues.create({
                owner: context.repo.owner,
                repo: context.repo.repo,
                title: `[Dashboard Review] ${item.folder} — Q${item.quarter}`,
                body: `Owner: @${item.approver}

Please review dashboards in \`${item.folder}\` and update the \`reviewed\` label in OWNERS to \`${item.quarter}\`.

Deadline: end of quarter.`,
                assignees: [item.approver],
                labels: ['dashboard-review'],
              });
            }
The Python script
find_stale_owners.py
reads the
reviewed
label from each OWNERS file and flags any folder where the label is more than one quarter old:
Usage Metrics and Stale Detection
Grafana exposes dashboard view counts via its internal stats API. A nightly job queries these counts and writes them as Prometheus metrics, enabling you to
alert on dashboards with zero views
over 60 days.

import requests, time
from prometheus_client import Gauge, push_to_gateway

GRAFANA_URL = "https://grafana.example.com"
GRAFANA_TOKEN = "..."          # service account token, read-only
PUSHGW = "prometheus-pushgw:9091"

dashboard_views = Gauge(
    'grafana_dashboard_views_total',
    'Number of dashboard views in the last 60 days',
    ['uid', 'title', 'folder', 'owner_team'],
)

def collect_dashboard_stats():
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{GRAFANA_URL}/api/search",
        params={"type": "dash-db", "limit": 1000},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {GRAFANA_TOKEN}"},
    )
    dashboards = resp.json()
    for d in dashboards:
        uid = d['uid']
        stats = requests.get(
            f"{GRAFANA_URL}/api/dashboards/uid/{uid}",
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {GRAFANA_TOKEN}"},
        ).json()
        views = stats.get('meta', {}).get('version', 0)   # approximation
        folder = d.get('folderTitle', 'General')
        # derive owner_team from folder path convention: teams/<team>/
        team = folder.split('/')[1] if folder.startswith('teams/') else 'unknown'
        dashboard_views.labels(
            uid=uid, title=d['title'], folder=folder, owner_team=team
        ).set(views)

collect_dashboard_stats()
push_to_gateway(PUSHGW, job='grafana-dashboard-stats', registry=None)
The corresponding Prometheus alert rule fires 30 days before a dashboard crosses the 60-day stale threshold, giving the owner time to either update the
reviewed
label or open a retirement PR:

# alerts/dashboard-staleness.yaml
groups:
  - name: dashboard_ownership
    rules:
      - alert: DashboardZeroViews
        expr: |
          grafana_dashboard_views_total == 0
          unless on(uid) grafana_dashboard_exempted{reason="intentional"}
        for: 30d
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: '{{ $labels.owner_team }}'
        annotations:
          summary: 'Dashboard {{ $labels.title }} has had zero views for 30+ days'
          description: |
            UID {{ $labels.uid }} in folder {{ $labels.folder }} has not been
            viewed in over 30 days. Owner: {{ $labels.owner_team }}.
            Consider retiring via PR or adding a grafana_dashboard_exempted metric.
Dashboard Retirement
A dashboard is a retirement candidate when:
Zero views in the last 60 days (detected by the staleness alert)
Its owner team has been disbanded or merged
Its feature was decommissioned
A newer dashboard supersedes it (e.g. v2 replaces v1)
The retirement process is deliberately slow to avoid accidental loss:
Open a PR that adds
deprecated: true
to the OWNERS file and tags the dashboard with a
deprecated
annotation in Jsonnet
The CI job deploys the
deprecated tag
to Grafana, which shows a banner in the UI
After
30 days
with no objections (no views, no comments on the issue), a second PR removes the
.jsonnet
file
The UID is placed on a
90-day hold list
before it can be reused — preventing stale bookmarks from pointing to a different dashboard

// Example: deprecation annotation added to dashboard
local deprecationAnnotation =
  g.dashboard.annotation.withName('DEPRECATED')
  + g.dashboard.annotation.withDatasource({ type: 'grafana', uid: '-- Grafana --' })
  + g.dashboard.annotation.withEnable(true)
  + g.dashboard.annotation.withHide(false)
  + g.dashboard.annotation.withIconColor('orange');

base
+ g.dashboard.withAnnotations([deprecationAnnotation])
+ g.dashboard.withDescription(
    'DEPRECATED: This dashboard is scheduled for removal on 2025-12-01. '
    + 'See github.com/org/repo/issues/1234 for the replacement.'
  )
Common Failure Modes
Failure
Symptom
Preventive measure
UID collision
Second deploy overwrites the first dashboard silently
CI unique-UID check on every PR
Orphaned dashboard
Dashboard exists in Grafana but not in git
GitOps deploy uses replace-all mode: any UID not in git is deleted
Stale query
Panel shows 'No data' after metric rename
Quarterly review + zero-data-panel alert
Over-permissive CODEOWNERS
Anyone can merge dashboard PRs
Branch protection requires ≥1 CODEOWNERS approver
Missing audience label
On-call engineers use finance dashboard during incident
CI lint enforces required OWNERS labels
Unreviewed fork
Team copies template but never registers ownership
CI rejects PRs to teams/ folders without an OWNERS file
Metrics for the Ownership Program Itself
Track the health of the ownership program with meta-metrics:
Percentage of dashboards with a current (this-quarter)
reviewed
label — target ≥ 90 %
Number of dashboards with zero views > 30 days — target trending down
Mean time from PR open to merge for dashboard changes — target < 2 business days
Number of dashboards deployed via GitOps vs created via UI — target 100 % GitOps
A Grafana dashboard for the ownership program itself (meta-dashboard) uses these metrics to give the observability lead a weekly health view:

# Fraction of dashboard folders with up-to-date reviewed label
sum(grafana_dashboard_reviewed_this_quarter == 1)
  /
count(grafana_dashboard_reviewed_this_quarter)
Summary
Dashboard review and ownership standards are what prevent an observability investment from becoming a maintenance burden. The key practices:
Every dashboard folder carries an OWNERS file with named approvers, reviewers, and a machine-readable audience label
PRs to dashboards require approval from both the observability lead and the team lead, enforced via GitHub CODEOWNERS
A CI checklist gates on UID uniqueness, variable references, cardinality, units, and SLO alignment before merge
Quarterly review issues are auto-opened for any folder where the reviewed label is more than one quarter old
Stale dashboards (zero views > 60 days) surface via Prometheus alerts and follow a 30-day deprecation + 90-day UID hold before retirement
A meta-dashboard tracks the health of the ownership program itself
